execution_info.prompt_template:
Now you are an expert who is good at using commonsense for reasoning.
The following conversation noted between '### ###' involves several speakers.
###
{history}
###
Based on the above historical utterances, please use commonsense to infer the **speaker's intention or reason** for saying < "{speaker_id}" : "{utterance}" >, with < {audio_features} >.
Output no more than 10 words:

inputs.history:
F: Yes.
M: Are you cold?  Do you want my jacket?  We should have brought the blanket, our blanket.
M: Oh, man.  This is great.  I didn't even think to bring a six pack.  Six pack would be just the ticket right about now.  I can't believe I forgot.  I'm like a kid.  It's amazing I got out of the house with my fly zipped.  Oh.
M: I wonder if they can hear me talk or feel the vibrations in the sand when we move around.
F: I'm cold.
M: You want my jacket?
F: It's after eleven; let's go home.
M: Are you kidding?  We'd miss it.  We just got here.  What's the point of coming down here if we just turn around and go home?
F: There is no point in coming down here.
M: Right.
F: That's my point.
M: That's my point, too.
F: So let's go home.
M: No, no, no.  That's not my point.  My point is is that there's no point in coming down here if we just leave.  Don't you want to see it?
F: Not particularly.
M: Why not?
F: I just don't.  It's stupid.
M: Honey, this is a natural phenomenon.  I mean, this only happens once a year.  It's a great opportunity.  Think about the people in Kansas.  They'd have to get plane tickets and hotel reservations to come see this.  We're just forty minutes away.  It's too good to pass up.
F: Pass up what?  These little fish swim onto the beach, flop around and then they swim away.  And then they die.
M: Yeah, but that moment on the beach is the highlight of their little lives.  I mean, all that flopping around is laying eggs or what do you -- spawning or mating, something like that.
M: There's mystery here and magic.
inputs.utterance: There's mystery here and magic.
inputs.audio_features: medium speech rate, medium pitch, and medium intensity

dev.output: To persuade F to stay and appreciate the natural phenomenon.

inputs.history:
F: When?
M: About four this morning.  I heard it cracking and I woke up and looked out.  He was standing right there when it cracked.
F: What was he doing out here at four in the morning?
M: I don't know.  After it cracked, he ran back in the house and cried in the kitchen.
F: Did you talk to him?
M: No.  I - I figured the best thing was to leave him alone.
F: He cried hard?
M: I heard him right through the floor of my room.
F: What was he doing out here at that hour?  He's dreaming about him again.  He's walking around at night.
M: I guess he is.
F: He's getting just like after he died.  What's the meaning in that?
M: I don't know the meaning of it, mom.  But I know one thing; we made a terrible mistake with dad.
F: What?
M: Being dishonest with him.  That kind of thing always pays off and now it's paying off.
F: What do you mean, dishonest?
M: You know that Larry's not coming back.  And I know it.  Why do we allow him to go on thinking that we believe with him?
F: What do you want to do?  Argue with him?
M: I don't want to argue with him but it's time he realized that nobody believes Larry's alive anymore.
M: Why shouldn't he dream of him?  Walk the nights waiting for him.
M: Do we contradict him?  Do we say straight out that we have no hope anymore?  That we haven't had any hope for years, now.
F: You can't say that to him.
inputs.utterance: You can't say that to him.
inputs.audio_features: medium speech rate, low pitch, and medium intensity

dev.output: To protect him from further pain and despair.

inputs.history:
F: Sure.  They're all told the same story.  It's what keeps us coming back for more; keeps us excited; keeps us thinking that life is going to start any minute now if we could just find the right spot; get in on the action.
M: Are we talking about the same thing?
F: Well, I'm sorry, but it's still just fish to me.
M: Carla, can I ask you something?  Don't take this the wrong way, but are you having your period?
F: Goddamn it, Augie, don't ask me that.  I hate it when you ask me that.  You always ask me that.  It's insulting.
M: Well what's bugging you?
F: We just have different ideas about what this evening is supposed to look like.
M: There's nothing to it.  We come down here.  The grunions arrive.  They do their little fish business.  We go ooh, ah, look at the fish.  They go home.  We go home.
F: For heaven's sake, we are not going to see the grunions.
M: Why not?
F: We never do, do we?  Have we ever?
M: We've missed them twice.
M: That's not ever.
F: Twice is every time we've tried.  That's ever.
M: We'll see them this year.
F: No, we won't.  It's pointless.  It's like waiting up to see Santa Claus.  I feel as though my entire life is going to be spent standing on the beach with my eyes wide open and my hands clasped expectantly waiting for the fish to show up.  And the fish won't show up.
M: I thought you wanted to see them.
F: You don't have any idea what I'm saying.
M: No, of course, not.
F: I did want to see it.  I always do.  I keep thinking this time will be the time, but it never is.
M: It's just fish; nothing to get traumatized over.
inputs.utterance: It's just fish; nothing to get traumatized over.
inputs.audio_features: medium speech rate, high pitch, and medium intensity

dev.output: To minimize F's emotional overreaction to the situation.

inputs.history:
M: Certainly you must.  We're figures of fun alright.
F: How long will it last, this ludicrous and overbearing love of ours?
M: Who knows.
F: Shall we always want to bicker and fight?
M: No.  That fire will fade along with our passion.
F: Oh, dear.  Should we like that.
M: It all depends on how well we've played.
F: What happens if one of us dies?  Does the one that's left, still laugh?
M: Yes.  Yes, with all his might.
F: Well that's serious enough, isn't it?
M: No, no, it isn't.  Death's very laughable; such a cunning little mystery, all done with mirrors.
F: Darling, I believe you're talking nonsense.
M: So is everyone else in the long run.  Let's be superficial and pity the poor philosophers.  Let's blow trumpets and squeakers and- and enjoy the party as much as we can, like very small, quite idiotic little school children.
M: Let's savor the delight of the moment.  Come and kiss me, darling, before your body rots and worms pop in and out of your eye sockets.
F: Elliot, worms don't pop.
M: I don't mind what you do, see?  You can paint yourself bright green all over and dance naked in the Place Vendome and rush off madly with all the men in the world and I shan't say a word as long as you love me best.
F: Thank you, dear.  The same applies to you, except that if I catch you so much as looking at another woman, I will kill you.
M: Do you remember that awful scene we had in Venice?
F: Which particular one?
M: The one when you bought that little painted wooded snake and put it on my bed?
F: Oh, Charles; that was his name, Charles.  Oh, he did wriggle so beautifully.
inputs.utterance: Oh, Charles; that was his name, Charles.  Oh, he did wriggle so beautifully.

inputs.history:

F: The manager came in and found is rolling about on the floor, biting and scratching like panthers.  Oh, dear.
M: Ah, I shall never forget his face.
F: How ridiculous. How utterly, utterly ridiculous.
M: Yes.  We were very much younger then.

inputs.history:
F: Pass up what?  These little fish swim onto the beach, flop around and then they swim away.  And then they die.

M: There's mystery here and magic.
M: Oh, it's a big night for these guys.  Uh, life's last orgy.
F: What are you so excited about?
F: This isn't a moon landing we're talking about.  It's fish.  It's fish nobody even eats.
M: Yeah, they do. Okay.  But it's fish who do something none of the other fish do; like the swallows are returning to Capistrano; or the lemmings throwing themselves off the cliffs, like that.
M: There's mystery here and magic; a little bit of the unexplainable.  I don't see how you can not be interested.
F: I'm just not.
M: For real?
F: Yes.
M: I don't get it.  First time we came here to see it, you said it was the best night of your life.  And last year, I remember distinctly you were so excited to get here, you stubbed your toe rushing me out the door and didn't even notice until we were in the car.
F: Last year, you will also remember, our shoes got wet.  I got sand in my panties.  We got into a big fight over nothing.  You got a sore throat and we did not see the grunions.
M: We weren't in a good spot.
F: We didn't see them the first time either.
M: We just weren't in a good spot.  This is a much better spot.  I've got good instincts for these things.  Look there.  What's that?  Is that -
M: no, it's just seaweed, isn't it?  Oh, man, this is great.  I wouldn't miss this for anything.  I mean, think about this.  I know you're not interested, but just think about this for a minute.
M: Out there someplace is this huge mass of silver fish heading this way.  They don't know why.  We don't know why.  It wasn't a decision.  They didn't take a vote.  No one sent them an invitation or a map;
M: just some little instinctual time release firecracker went off in their little bodies and all as one, they turned and formed a line as long as the California coast and started swimming.
M: They're swimming right now as if that's the only thing that matters.  It's single mindedness with no mind.  Urgency that's pure urge.
M: They don't know what drives them.  They don't give it a name or ask a question.  They just turn, as one, and start the swim that brings them here; to this place, where we stand, for a massive celebration of sex and death.  Oh, man, I'm giving myself goose bumps.  All my hairs are popping up.

F: Darling, I believe you're talking nonsense.

F: When?

F: What was he doing out here at four in the morning?
M: I don't know.  After it cracked, he ran back in the house and cried in the kitchen.
F: Did you talk to him?

F: What do you mean, dishonest?
